coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/graphene hybrid nanocomposite as an efficient catalyst support for the oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Jianyu
Hu, Yue
Chen, Lijuan
Xu, Juan
Chen, Zhidong
coredata.dc:description: A nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/graphene (N-CQD/G) hybrid nanocomposite was synthesized in-situ using a sacrificial template-assisted pyrolysis method with an oligomeric phenolic resin as the carbon source and dicyandiamide as the sacrificial template precursor/nitrogen source. Scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, atomic force microscopy, nitrogen adsorption–desorption, Raman and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy were employed to characterize N-CQD/G samples. The Pt nanoparticles were then deposited onto N-CQD/G hybrids (Pt/N-CQD/G) using a polyol reduction method. Structural and morphological investigations for the Pt/N-GQD/G revealed that ultrafine Pt particles with an average size of 2.1 nm, together with CQDs, are uniformly dispersed on the surfaces of graphene nanosheets. Compared with commercial Pt/C catalysts, Pt/N-CQD/G with highly electrochemical active surface area (ca. 123.4 m2 g−1 Pt) show remarkably enhanced electrocatalytic activity and durability toward oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). The superior ORR catalytic performance of Pt/N-CQD/G can be attributed to highly distributed Pt nanoparticles with small size, nitrogen-doping, and strong interaction between metal and support, as well as the unique structure of N-CQD/G hybrids which combines the advantages of both carbon quantum dot and graphene, such as abundant edges and doping sites, high surface area, large pore size and high electrical conductivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Nitrogen doping
Sacrificial template
Electrocatalyst
Oxygen reduction reaction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issfirst issn issnnorm issuelist itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and structure of N-CQD/G. (a, b) FESEM image, (c) TEM image, (d) HRTEM image, (e) CQD particle size distribution, and (f) XRD pattern of N-CQD/G.
TEM images (a, b) of the product obtained from pyrolysis of phenolic resin without using dicyandiamide. The inset in (b) is the HRTEM image of the CQDs.
AFM image of N-CQD/G and the corresponding height analyses along the line marked in the AFM image.
N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (a) and Raman spectrum (b) for N-CQD/G. The inset of Fig. 4a is the BJH pore size distribution.
Morphology and structure of Pt/N-CQD/G. (a) TEM image, (b, c) HRTEM images, and (d) XRD pattern of Pt/N-CQD/G.
CVs on the Pt/N-CQD/G and commercial Pt/C electrode in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1.

(a) RDE linear sweep voltammograms on the Pt/N-CQD/G and commercial Pt/C electrodes at a rotating speed of 1600 rpm in O2-saturated 0.1 M HClO4 solution with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. The potentials of the Pt/N-CQD/G and commercial Pt/C electrodes were corrected for the solution resistance (Rs). (b) RDE linear sweep voltammograms on the Pt/N-CQD/G electrode at different rotating speeds in O2-saturated 0.1 M HClO4 solution. (c) Koutecký–Levich plots of the Pt/N-CQD/G derived from RDE voltammograms in (b) at different potentials. (d) The electrocatalytic activity given as the kinetic current density at 0.85 V and average electron transfer number for the Pt/N-CQD/G and commercial Pt/C.
High-resolution XPS Pt 4f spectra of the Pt/N-CQD/G and commercial Pt/C.

Electrochemical durability of the Pt/N-CQD/G and commercial Pt/C catalyst. LSV curves of (a) the Pt/N-CQD/G catalyst and (b) commercial Pt/C catalyst before and after 5000 potential cycles between 0.6 and 1.2 V vs. RHE. The durability tests were carried out at room temperature in O2-saturated 0.1 M HClO4 at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis procedures of N-CQD/G hybrid materials.
Structural and electrochemical characterizations for the Pt/N-CQD/G and commercial Pt/C catalysts, in comparison to those in several recent studies. NA, not applicable.
Half-wave potential (E1/2) and mass activity (MA) of the Pt/N-CQD/G and commercial Pt/C catalysts, in comparison to those in several recent studies. NA, not applicable.

row.entry: MA at 0.85 V (A g−1) after 5000 cycles

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/graphene hybrid nanocomposite as an efficient catalyst support for the oxygen reduction reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Photovoltaic Science and Engineering, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Photovoltaic Science and Engineering
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot/graphene (N-CQD/G) hybrid nanocomposite was synthesized in-situ using a sacrificial template-assisted pyrolysis method with an oligomeric phenolic resin as the carbon source and dicyandiamide as the sacrificial template precursor/nitrogen source. Scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, atomic force microscopy, nitrogen adsorption–desorption, Raman and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy were employed to characterize N-CQD/G samples. The Pt nanoparticles were then deposited onto N-CQD/G hybrids (Pt/N-CQD/G) using a polyol reduction method. Structural and morphological investigations for the Pt/N-GQD/G revealed that ultrafine Pt particles with an average size of 2.1 nm, together with CQDs, are uniformly dispersed on the surfaces of graphene nanosheets. Compared with commercial Pt/C catalysts, Pt/N-CQD/G with highly electrochemical active surface area (ca. 123.4 m2 g−1 Pt) show remarkably enhanced electrocatalytic activity and durability toward oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). The superior ORR catalytic performance of Pt/N-CQD/G can be attributed to highly distributed Pt nanoparticles with small size, nitrogen-doping, and strong interaction between metal and support, as well as the unique structure of N-CQD/G hybrids which combines the advantages of both carbon quantum dot and graphene, such as abundant edges and doping sites, high surface area, large pore size and high electrical conductivity.

ce:section.ce:para: The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), as a fundamental reaction, plays an important role in fuel cells and rechargeable metal-air batteries [1,2]. However, due to the intrinsically sluggish kinetics, efficient ORR catalysts are urgently required [2]. In recent years, extensive research effort is aimed at exploring low-cost nonprecious metal (NPM) catalysts (e.g. FeNC and CoNC) with ORR activity comparable to that of platinum in acidic medium [3–5]. Completely eliminating platinum from ORR catalysts is preferred but has been highly challenging because of few candidate elements to meet the requirement of high ORR activity and durability in acidic medium [6–8]. Despite a significant breakthrough in NPM catalysts was achieved recently, the state-of-the art NPM catalysts reported by Dodelet et al. only improved their activity to about 1/10 that of commercial Pt/C catalysts and presented inferior catalytic durability [5]. Up to now, Pt-based nanomaterials are still the most efficient ORR catalyst in acidic medium. Lots of considerable approaches such as alloying with non-precious metals [9,10], controllably synthesizing core–shell nanoparticles with thin Pt skin layer [11,12], and employing carbon nanomaterials or a hybrid composite of those as supports to uniformly disperse Pt nanoparticles [13,14] have been utilized to enhance the catalytic efficiency of Pt catalysts and substantially reduce their costs. Carbon is the most widely used support material of electro-catalysts because of its excellent chemical inertness, high electronic conductivity, large surface area, and appropriate pore structures. Various kinds of nanocarbons, such as carbon nanotubes [13], nanospheres [15], nanocoils [16], nanowires [17], nanohorns [18], graphene [19] and graphdiyne [20] had been successfully synthesized and used as supports for fuel cell catalysts. Moreover, nanocarbons can be modified easily by introducing of heteroatoms (e.g. N, B, and S) [21–25] or a combination of different heteroatoms [26,27] into graphite basal layers to improve catalytic activity of supported metal catalysts due to the fact that the embedded heteroatoms can significantly alter the local spin or charge density by inducing an uneven charge distribution in the adjacent sites, thus enhancing electronic conductivity and promoting adsorption and activation of molecular oxygen [28].

Recently, great developments have been made on carbon-based quantum dots. Carbon-based quantum dot materials were divided into two categories, namely carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). As a new class of zero-dimensional nanocarbons with sizes below 10 nm, CQDs and/or GQDs are associated with quantum-confinement and edge effects, leading to wide applications in solar cells, electrochemical capacitors, light-emitting diodes (LEDs), drug delivery, fluorescent bioimaging, and sensors [29–31]. The edge-abundant features of carbon-based quantum dots are greatly beneficial for electrochemical catalysis as reactions are more readily catalysed at the edge planes than the basal planes [32]. However, when nitrogen-doped GQDs were employed to electrocatalyze ORR, the improvement in electrocatalytic activity was found to be extremely limited due to the low conductivity of the electrode made of small GQDs [21,33]. Accordingly, combining the advantages of both GQD and graphene, Fei et al. [26] proposed to synthesize BN-codoped GQD/graphene (BN-GQD/G) hybrid materials by self-assembling GQDs synthesized from coal on graphene oxides via a hydrothermal approach and then co-doping with nitrogen and boron by thermal annealing treatment in a chemical vapour deposition (CVD) oven. The BN-GQD/G hybrid presents an excellent ORR activity in alkaline media. However, this hybrid material has not been reported to be used as the support of electrocatalysts or to catalyse ORR in acidic media. In addition, complicated manipulation, high production cost, and the use of corrosive reagents in the preparation process limit their practical application. Therefore, it would be highly desirable to develop a facile and cost-efficient strategy to prepare carbon-based quantum dots/graphene hybrid materials for electrocatalysis application. In this work, we successfully synthesized in-situ nitrogen-doped CQD/graphene (N-CQD/G) hybrid nanocomposites by a facile sacrificial-template-assisted pyrolysis strategy. The N-CQD/G supported Pt nanocatalysts (Pt/N-CQD/G) were then prepared by uniformly deposited Pt nanoparticles onto N-CQD/G hybrids using a facile polyol reduction method. Being used as an ORR electrocatalyst, the Pt/N-CQD/G shows superior electrocatalytic activity and durability, compared with the commercial Pt/C in acidic media, which makes it a promising catalyst in the electrochemical application.

Dicyandiamide (DCDA) and β-cyclodextrin were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Glucose, ethylene glycol (EG), sodium citrate and perchloric acid (HClO4) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Sodium hexachloroplatinate (Na2PtCl6) was purchased from Alfa-Aesar. All chemicals were all analytical reagents and used as received without further purification. Commercially Pt/C catalysts (20 wt.% Pt loading) were purchased from Johnson Matthey (JM).

The brief procedures were described as follows: 0.1 g powdered oligomeric phenolic resin synthesized according to a method reported previously [34] was uniformly mixed with dicyandiamide (4.0 g) in a mortar by grinding. The resulting powdered mixture was then transferred into a quartz crucible with a lid, which was placed at the central segment of a long quartz tube in a pyrolysis furnace. The furnace was heated to 1000 °C with an ultra-pure N2 gas flowing at ca. 100 mL min−1 and then kept at this temperature for 1 h. During this procedure, the heating rate before and after 600 °C was 2 and 3.3 °C min−1, respectively. The furnace was cooled to room temperature, slowly. The final product was collected from the crucible directly. For a comparison, the samples synthesized under the same conditions using glucose or β-cyclodextrin as the carbon source, were labelled as NG-G and NG-CD, respectively. Pt/N-CQD/G catalysts were prepared by a previously reported polyol reduction method with EG as the reductant and sodium citrate as the complexing agent/stabilizer [35]. The total Pt loading was controlled at 20 wt.%.

The morphology of samples was observed by field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Supra 55, Carl Zeiss), transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL), and atomic force microscope (AFM, Multimode V, Veeco). Surface compositions were analysed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250) using Al Kα radiation. Collected data were corrected for charge shifting using standard C1s binding energy of 284.5 eV. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) measurements were carried out using a Rigaku D/max-2500 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm). Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms were obtained by a gas adsorption analyser (ASAP2010, Micromeritics Instrument Co.) at 77 K. Before measurements, the samples were degassed in a vacuum at 200 °C for 6 h. Specific surface area and pore volume were determined using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method and the single point method, respectively. Pore size distribution (PSD) curve was obtained according to Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) model. The position of the maximum of the PSD was used as the average pore diameter. The Raman spectra were collected using a Raman microscope (DXR, Thermo Scientific) with an excitation wavelength of 514 nm. The bulk electrical conductivity was measured with a powder resistivity meter (FZ-9601, Shanghai Xiyu Co. Ltd.) by confining the pressed samples between two metal discs. During the conductivity measurement, a pressure of 2 MPa is applied to ensure good electrical contacts between carbon grains.

First, 8 mg of Pt/N-CQD/G (or Pt/C) samples was dispersed into a mixture composed of 5 wt.% Nafion solution (0.15 mL), isopropanol (1 mL), and ultrapure water (1 mL) by at least 30 min of sonication to form a homogeneous ink. Then, 8 μL of this ink was transferred onto a freshly polished glassy carbon rotating disk electrode (GC RDE, 0.1963 cm2), and left to dry overnight to serve as a working electrode [35]. Pt loading at the working electrode was ca. 30.3 μg cm−2. The saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was used as a reference and a platinum foil served as the counter electrode. All the potentials reported herein were calibrated with respect to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) according to the Nernst equation. CHI 660B potentiostat (CH Instruments Inc.) and PAR RDE speed control unit (Model 616) were employed for the electrochemical analyses and the RDE experiments. ORR measurements were examined in 0.1 M HClO4 solution saturated with O2 at 25 ± 1 °C.

The N-CQD/G hybrid materials were synthesized using a sacrificial template-assisted pyrolysis method reported previously [36] with an oligomeric phenolic resin as the carbon source. The preparation details are illustrated in Scheme 1 . Here, dicyandiamide acts as a sacrificial template precursor/nitrogen source, undergoing a stepwise thermal polymerization and finally forms g-C3N4 polymers in the pyrolysis procedure. The as-formed g-C3N4 decomposed rapidly when the temperature above 600 °C, which can create abundant pores and promote the formation of graphene structure.

The morphology of the resulting N-CQD/G hybrid materials was examined by FESEM and TEM images. FESEM images shown in Fig. 1 a and b clearly reveal three-dimensional (3D) well-integrated nanoporous architectures throughout the entire sample. The pore size ranges from several tens of nanometers to more than one hundred nanometers. The TEM image (Fig. 1c) of N-CQD/G hybrid shows that lots of nanoparticles (3–9 nm in size) are uniformly decorated on flat graphene nanosheets (marked by the red arrow), suggesting that the honeycomb-like nanoporous structure shown in Fig. 1b is constructed by graphene lamellae. These nanoparticles attached onto graphene sheets can be easily inferred as carbon dots since there are only three elements (namely, C, O, and N) in the N-CQD/G sample, revealed by the wide scan XPS spectrum as well as high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. S1). However, it is noted that no distinct CQDs were observed in TEM images (shown in Fig. S2) of those nitrogen-doped graphenes (NGs) synthesized under the same pyrolysis condition using glucose or β-cyclodextrin as the carbon source (NG-G and NG-CD), indicating that the formation of CQDs is templating agent (dicyandiamide) independent and correlates with the type of carbon source. In order to further confirm this speculation, we synthesized a carbon sample by direct-pyrolysis of the oligomeric phenolic resin without using dicyandiamide under the same pyrolysis condition. TEM characterization reveals that tiny CQDs (3–7 nm), carbon nanospheres (20–50 nm), and graphene nanosheets were observed within this carbon sample (Fig. 2 a). Some well-dispersive CQDs with a lattice spacing of ∼0.34 nm, which agrees with lattice spacing of the (002) facet of graphite, can be clearly found in their high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shown in Fig. 2b. It can be concluded from the above results that CQDs and graphene nanosheets within N-CQD/G are derived from different molecular weight of phenolic polymer. Phenolic polymers of low molecular weight may preferentially form zero-dimensional quantum dots while large phenolic polymers would be first thermally polymerized and then decomposed to form graphene nanosheets in the pyrolysis preparation. The wall thickness of porous graphene lamellae can be measured (10 places were measured) from the HRTEM image of N-CQD/G (Fig. 1d). The majority pore walls are thinner than 10 nm, and the average thickness is estimated to be ∼4.7 nm. The HRTEM image of N-CQD/G further reveals that porous graphene lamellae is well-crystallized, and sharp edges made of a graphitic layer with a (002) plane corresponding to a d spacing of ∼0.36 nm can be observed clearly, slightly larger than that of crystalline graphite (0.335 nm). The average diameter of CQDs within the N-CQD/G is about 4.6 nm, with a relatively narrow size distribution between 3.0 and 8.6 nm (Fig. 1e). These tiny CQDs attached onto graphene nanosheets create abundant edge defects, favourable to disperse uniformly catalytic metal nanoparticles. AFM scanning image was given in Fig. 3 , revealing again the coexistence of CQDs and graphene nanosheets. The thickness for the individual CQD and graphene nanosheet was found to be ca. 4.4 and 4.2 nm, respectively, consistent with HRTEM results. The XRD pattern (shown in Fig. 1f) of N-CQD/G reveals a broad peak of (002) diffraction around 25.0°, corresponding to the interlayer spacing of ∼0.356 nm. This value is in good agreement with the HRTEM results. The broadening of (002) peak indicates the distribution of interlayer distance in N-CQD/G due to the presence of abundant surface defects and residual functional groups [37]. In addition, a weak (101) peak at ∼43.5° can be observed, suggesting the formation of graphite basal layer for N-CQD/G hybrid [38].

The highly porous nanostructure for the N-CQD/G is also revealed by N2 adsorption–desorption results. The typical type IV isotherms illustrated in Fig. 4 a clearly show the presence of abundant mesopores within N-CQD/G, which are created by the formation and subsequent decomposition of g-C3N4 sacrificial template [34]. The BJH pore size distribution (the inset of Fig. 4a) shows that the pore size for N-CQD/G ranges from 3 to 40 nm, with an average pore size of 10.8 nm, revealing its mesoporous characteristics. The specific surface area of N-CQD/G is calculated to be 801.2 m2 g−1, about 1/3 of the theoretical surface area of single-layered graphene (2630 m2 g−1) [39], which is comparable with that of CVD-generated graphenes (∼850 m2 g−1) on Ni foam [40], and is much higher than that of 2D mesoporous graphene reported previously [41]. The Raman spectrum of N-CQD/G displays two remarkable peaks at around 1350 and 1585 cm−1 (Fig. 4b) corresponding to the D band and G band, respectively. The G band related to the E2g vibration mode of sp2 carbon domains can be used to suggest the degree of graphitization, while the D band is associated with structural defects and partially disordered structures of the sp2 domains [42]. The intensity ratio of two bands (ID/IG) is widely used to prove the graphitization degree of carbon materials. The ID/IG value for N-CQD/G is found to be ∼1.43, which is much higher than that of NGs obtained by chemical vapour deposition (ID/IG ∼0.25 or even lower) [43], but close to those of NGs (1.07–1.28) synthesized via a similar pyrolysis approach [34,44]. The large ratio of ID/IG suggests that N-CQD/G possesses abundant surface defects. The position and shape of 2D peak in the Raman spectrum is often used to distinguish the number of graphene layers. In Fig. 4b, the 2D peaks appear at around 2700 cm−1 is very weak and almost cannot be observed clearly, combined with TEM and AFM results, revealing that as-made N-CQD/G is composed of multilayered graphene architectures [45].

XPS was used to determine the doping content and chemical state of elements in N-CQD/G hybrids. The distinct C1s and O1s peaks as well as weak N1s signals were observed in the wide scan XPS spectrum (Fig. S1a), suggesting that as-synthesized N-CQD/G was completely metal-free. The element content in N-CQD/G can be calculated from the corresponding peak areas and relative sensitivity factors to be ca. 77.1 at.% carbon, 2.4 at.% nitrogen, and 20.5 at.% oxygen. The C1s spectrum (Fig. S1b) of N-CQD/G shows a main peak at ∼284.5 eV (C1), corresponding to sp2-hybridized graphitic carbon atoms and small signals at higher binding energy indicating some CN/CO (C2, 286.2 eV) and NCO/OCO (C3, 288.5 eV) species remaining in the sample. The XPS N1s spectrum (Fig. S1c) of N-CQD/G can be deconvoluted into five peaks assignable to cyano nitrogen (N1, 396.5–397.3 eV), pyridinic nitrogen (N2, 398.3–398.4 eV), pyrrolic nitrogen (N3, 400.3–401.1 eV), graphitic nitrogen (N4, 402.0–402.7 eV), and oxidized species of nitrogen (N5, 405.0–405.4 eV), respectively [46]. Among these nitrogen species, pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen represent a class of nitrogen atoms that bound to the edges of graphite layers, being generally considered to be located in a π-conjugated system and contribute to the aromatic π system with one or two electrons, respectively. Graphitic nitrogen refers to nitrogen atoms replacing carbon atoms in the graphitic lattice with the excess electron delocalized in the π system, which may improve the electronic conductivity [47–49]. The bulk conductivity of the N-CQD/G at the room temperature was measured to be ca. 156.2 S cm−1, much higher than that of the commercial carbon support – Vulcan XC72R (ca. 3.4 S cm−1), revealing that the N-CQD/G is a carbon material possessing superior electrical conductivity properties. In the N-CQD/G architecture, porous graphene nanosheets not only may promote the uniform distribution of CQDs, but also act as conductive substrates, interconnecting the CQDs and then facilitating electron transfer due to their high electrical conductivity. The CQDs themselves are too small to construct a sufficient percolative network to maintain high conductivity [26]. In addition, the honeycomb-like nanoporous network formed by N-CQD/G hybrid allows rapid transport of electrolyte, reactants and products [2,15,22]. More importantly, the CQDs in-situ formed from polymer precursors possess abundant exposed edges and defects as well as nitrogen-containing groups, which can facilitate uniform dispersion and immobilization of Pt particles on the hybrid supports and make the surface more chemically active for the enhanced Pt-support interaction [50].

The Pt nanoparticles were deposited onto the N-CQD/G hybrid (Pt/N-CQD/G) using a polyol reduction method. The morphology and structure of Pt/N-CQD/G were examined by TEM and XRD characterizations. Typical TEM image of Pt/N-CQD/G is presented in Fig. 5 a, in which tiny nanoparticles are observed uniformly distributed on crumpled graphene nanosheets [51]. The HRTEM images of the Pt/N-CQD/G sample (Fig. 5b and c) show the formation of well-dispersive Pt nanocrystals with a lattice spacing of ∼0.227 nm, in good agreement with the lattice spacing of the (111) facet of face-centered cubic (fcc) platinum [52]. Besides, some small CQDs were also found, with the lattice spacings of ∼0.332 and ∼0.204 nm, corresponding to (002) and (101) facets of hexagonal graphite, respectively, revealing that Pt nanoparticles and CQDs coexist in the Pt/N-CQD/G sample. In the XRD pattern of the Pt/N-CQD/G (Fig. 5d), characteristic diffraction peaks for (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes were observed clearly, which further confirmed the fcc structure of Pt nanocrystals on the N-CQD/G (PDF card 04-0802). All these peaks were slightly shifted to lower 2θ angles compared with pure Pt (PDF No. 04-0802). This small shift of 2θ angle may be derived from the broadening of diffraction peak resulted from the extremely small size of Pt nanoparticles, which causes slight biases of the peak position [53,54]. The significant broadening in XRD diffraction peaks revealed very small size of Pt nanocrystals. According to the Sherrer formula, the average size of Pt nanocrystals in the Pt/N-CQD/G was determined to be ca. 2.1 nm, much smaller than that of JM Pt/C catalysts (ca. 2.8 nm) [35].

The cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of the Pt/N-CQD/G and commercial Pt/C in a 0.5 M H2SO4 solution were presented in Fig. 6 . Prior to the measurements, these catalytic electrodes were activated by continuous CV scans between −0.2 and 1.0 V (vs SCE) with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 for 50 cycles, in order to achieve stable CV curves. Electrochemical surface areas (ECSAs) for the catalysts were calculated by integrating hydrogen adsorption charges [55]. The specific ECSA for the Pt/N-CQD/G was measured to be ca. 123.4 m2 g−1 Pt, compared with ca. 67.6 m2 g−1 Pt for the commercial Pt/C. This enhancement in ECSA of the Pt/N-CQD/G may be attributed to the fact that extremely small Pt nanoparticles (∼2.1 nm) attached on porous N-CQD/G hybrids engender a large number of catalytically active sites. The geometric surface areas (GSAs) for Pt nanoparticles in the Pt/N-CQD/G and commercial Pt/C were calculated using the equation of S = 6/(ρd), based on the assumption of a spherical morphology, where S is the GSA in m2 g−1, ρ the density of bulk Pt (21.4 gcm−3), and d the diameter of the Pt particle in nm determined from the XRD patterns, respectively [35]. The Pt utilization efficiency was obtained by dividing the ECSA by GSA. As shown in Table 1 , the Pt utilization efficiency of the Pt/N-CQD/G was determined to be as high as 92.4% while that of the commercial Pt/C was 66.9%, which is also higher than those of previously reported Pt catalysts [56–59], demonstrating again the importance of the N-CQD/G support. Polarization curves of the Pt/N-CQD/G and commercial Pt/C in O2-saturated 0.1 M HClO4 solution were recorded on a rotating disk electrode at a rotating speed of 1600 rpm (Fig. 7 a). The onset potential and half-wave potential values are important parameters to evaluate the elctrocatalytic activity of catalysts for ORR, and more positive onset potential and half-wave potential indicate the enhanced elctrocatalytic activity for ORR. As illustrated in Fig. 7a, the onset potential for ORR on the Pt/N-GQD/G catalyst is 0.976 V, more positive as compared to 0.941 V observed on commercial Pt/C. The Pt/N-CQD/G catalyst also shows a higher half-wave potential (0.880 V), compared with that of the commercial Pt/C (0.822 V), indicating a remarkable enhancement of ORR activity when Pt nanoparticles are loaded on N-CQD/G support. All the above information reveals that N-CQD/G hybrid nanocomposites as the support can facilitate the oxygen reduction rate. The ORR kinetics was investigated by RDE voltammetry technique (Fig. 7b). The Koutecký–Levich plots at various potentials (Fig. 7c) for the Pt/N-GQD/G revealed a distinctly parallel feature with a good linearity at different potentials, which are generally considered as an indication of first-order reaction kinetics with respect to the concentration of dissolved oxygen [60]. The average electron transfer number (n) presented in Fig. 7d was obtained from slopes of Koutecký–Levich plots at different potentials. The average n value is determined to be 3.7 for the Pt/N-GQD/G sample, very close to that of the commercial Pt/C (3.8), suggesting that the Pt/N-GQD/G leads to a four-electron transfer in oxygen reduction process. Mass-transport-corrected kinetic current density at 0.85 V was calculated according to the Koutecký–Levich equation: 1/j = 1/j k + 1/j d, where j k is the kinetic current density and j d is the diffusion-limiting current density [61]. As shown in Fig. 7d, the specific activities of the Pt/N-GQD/G at 0.85 V are 12.72 mA cm−2, over six times that of the commercially Pt/C (2.01 mA cm−2). Converted into mass activity, the Pt/N-GQD/G possesses a mass activity of 420 A g−1 Pt, much higher than that of the commercial Pt/C (66.3 A g−1 Pt), indicating the obvious enhancement of catalytic activity when the N-CQD/G is utilized as the support.

To gain a further insight into the effect of the N-CQD/G support on catalytic activity, Pt4f core level XPS spectra of the Pt/N-CQD/G and commercial Pt/C were recorded, which were presented in Fig. 8 . The smaller doublets around 72.4 (Pt 4f7/2) and 75.6 eV (Pt 4f5/2) for the Pt/N-CQD/G can be assigned to Pt(II) chemical states [62]. As compared with the Pt/C, the most intense doublet peaks at 71.0 (Pt 4f7/2) and 74.3 eV (Pt 4f5/2) assigned to zero-valent platinum (Pt0) [63] for the Pt/N-GQD/G, were found to slightly shift toward higher binding energies (71.2 and 74.6 eV), which was also observed in some previous reports [64,65]. The XPS signals for Pt(IV) species are not directly observed in the Pt4f spectrum of the Pt/N-CQD/G. It is reported previously by Cuong and Chi [66] that the electron in the d orbital of Pt can be shared with a π* antibonding orbital from the carbon support by π back-bonding, resulting in a partially covalent nature of the binding between Pt clusters and the carbon support. Therefore, this upshift of binding energy for Pt on N-CQD/G is ascribed to an enhanced Pt-support interaction due to synergistical π back-bonding, which may alter the electronic structure of Pt and lead to the improvement of catalytic performance [67,68]. In addition, the percentage of Pt species was calculated from the relative intensities of these peaks [69,70], and the results are summarized in Table S1. The Pt/N-CQD/G possesses 50.7% Pt0, higher than that of the Pt/C (44.2%). Previous studies have indicated that the decrease in the size of Pt particles also might cause a positive shift of Pt4f core-level binding energy [15]. However, generally this size effect would simultaneously be accompanied by a decrease of Pt0 species on the surface with decreasing size of Pt particle [62]. Since the decrease of Pt0 concentration was not observed in the Pt/N-CQD/G with much smaller Pt particle size compared to that of the JM Pt/C catalyst, we believe that this positive shift of Pt4f binding energy is mainly due to the increased interaction between metal particles and supports.

In general, the remarkable enhancement in electrocatalytic activity for ORR using N-doped CQD/graphene hybrid supported Pt catalysts can be explained by the following three principles: (i) loaded tiny Pt nanoparticles with well-dispersed morphology contribute to a significant improvement in Pt utilization, (ii) stronger metal-support interaction and better conductivity originated from N-doping facilitate charge carrier transfer, and (iii) the nanoporous 3D network structure allows rapid transport of electrolyte, reactants and products. To gain insight into durability, an accelerated durability test (ADT) of electrocatalysts was carried out by cycling between 0.6 and 1.2 V for 5000 potential cycles at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 in O2-saturated 0.1 M HClO4. Fig. 9 presented the ORR activities of the Pt/N-CQD/G and commercial Pt/C catalyst before and after 5000 potential cycles. The mass activities of Pt/N-CQD/G and Pt/C catalysts before and after 5000 cycles were listed in Table 2 , in comparison to those in several recent studies [69,71–74]. As can be seen in the table, the mass activity of the Pt/N-CQD/G for ORR was found to decrease by 23.6% from 420 to 321 A g−1 Pt after 5000 cycles, whereas the Pt/C showed a loss of ∼30.8% of its initial activity (from 66.3 to 45.9 A g−1 Pt). The Pt/N-CQD/G showed almost seven-fold higher ORR activity than the Pt/C catalyst after the ADT experiment.

In this study, nitrogen-doped CQD/graphene hybrid materials were successfully prepared using a sacrificial template-assisted pyrolysis strategy, where in-situ formed tiny CQDs attached onto graphene nanosheets to make a nanoporous 3D network structure. The CQDs onto graphene nanosheets together with nitrogen-doping can effectively modify the surface states and structures of carbon materials showing positive physical and electrochemical properties as the support material. Pt nanoparticles supported on such carbon/carbon hybrids show a superior electrocatalytic activity toward the ORR with the more positive half-wave potential as well as much higher kinetic current density through a four-electron reduction mechanism, compared to the commercial Pt/C. Furthermore, this catalyst exhibits a superior ORR durability after 5000 potential cycles, better than that of the commercial Pt/C catalyst. Well-dispersed Pt nanoparticles, nitrogen-doping, the 3D nanoporous structure of N-CQD/G, and strong interaction between metal and support may be all crucial factors for the superior catalytic behaviour of Pt/N-CQD/G. Besides the promising performance at oxygen reduction, N-CQD/G materials can be expected to provide wide applications in the field of lithium ion batteries, electrochemical capacitors, and other energy storage and conversion. The present synthesis method can also be extended to prepare nitrogen and other heteroatom (B, P, S, and halogen) co-doped CQD/graphene hybrid materials if appropriate precursors are utilized.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21573025 and 51574047), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20151183), Foundation of Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology (BM2012110), Qing Lan Project, and Foundation of Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center, Changzhou University (ACGM2016-06-30) for support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2017.01.029.
